sub: Why aviation software is built differently — and what drones inherit from it

title: The Standards Landscape
desc: Who writes the rules for safety-critical aviation software

rows: RTCA (Radio Technical Commission for Aeronautics, USA)
Software considerations in airborne systems — the certification standard for flight software
EUROCAE (European Organisation for Civil Aviation Equipment)
Software integrity assurance for ground and ATM systems (European counterpart of DO-278)
Software safety assurance for air navigation service (ANS) systems — air traffic systems
System-level development of civil aircraft — allocates Development Assurance Levels (DALs) to software
Military system safety — allocates a Level of Rigour, translatable into a DO-178 DAL

title: Safety Culture Predates Software
desc: Aviation learned about redundancy, checklists and failure analysis long before code existed

body.alt: Ground crew preparing a home-built ultralight aircraft, 1986
body.caption: 1986: even the ultralight my father built by hand got a rigorous pre-flight inspection. The mindset — assume failure, check everything, control change — is exactly what software safety standards later formalized.

items: Aviation safety engineering matured over a century of accidents and investigations
Software safety standards are that culture, translated into the language of code: requirements, traceability, verification evidence
Drones now inherit both worlds — and the standards that come with them

title: The Economics of Fixing Defects
desc: Why standards front-load so much work into requirements and design

body.alt: Bar chart: relative cost of fixing defects rises from 1 at design to 100 in maintenance

body.card: Relative cost of fixing a defect
items: Found during design: cost 1×
Found during implementation: 6.5×
Found during testing: 15×
Found in maintenance (i.e. in the field): 100×
Source: Dawson, Burrell, Rahim & Brewster (2010), Integrating Software Assurance into the SDLC
In aviation, 'found in the field' can mean an accident investigation — the 100× becomes incalculable

title: 'Perfect' Software Can Kill
desc: Correct code is not the same as a safe system

body.alt: Pie chart of computer-related accidental deaths: 91.9% human-computer interaction problems

items: 91.9% — human-computer interaction problems
4.5% — physical causes
2.8% — software errors
0.8% — other
The software can meet every requirement and still contribute to a fatal accident — if the requirements or the interaction design are wrong
Source: Donald MacKenzie, Computer-related accidental death, Science and Public Policy 21(4), 1994

title: 'Out of Control' — Where Failures Are Born
desc: UK Health & Safety Executive study of control system failures (HSG 238, 2003)

body.alt: Pie chart of control system failure causes by lifecycle phase, specification dominates with 44%

items: 44% — specification
20% — changes after commissioning
15% — design and implementation
15% — operation and maintenance
6% — installation and commissioning
Almost two thirds of failures are created before or after the code is written — in the spec, or in uncontrolled change

title: Safe Software Design: What It Is (and Isn't)
desc: The uncomfortable conclusion from the failure statistics

body.card: Safe software design ISN'T so much about:
items: Design and coding
Fault counts in code
Unit testing
Understanding context — what system, what hazards, what environment
Requirements / specification — where 44% of failures are born
Understanding the human-control interaction — where 91.9% of fatal problems live
Controlling change — the second-largest failure source

title: Trust Levels & Categories: SIL
desc: Safety Integrity Levels grade how much you must trust the software

body.alt: Table of SIL levels 0-4 with requirements for specification, languages, configuration control, testing and assessment

items: SIL 0 = ISO 9001 process only — no safety claim at all; an accident is quite likely to happen
SIL 1–2 — structured methods, controlled changes, functional testing and reviews
SIL 3–4 — formal specification, restricted language subsets, validated/proven compilers, static analysis up to 100% testing & proof
The higher the level, the stronger the claim: at the top end, this software may hold human lives

title: Principle 1: Define the Safety Requirements
desc: Software safety requirements must address the software's contribution to system hazards

items: Software safety requirements must be defined to consider the software's contribution to system hazards
DO-178B/C assumes a system-level safety analysis has already derived safety requirements and allocated them to software
A system-level standard judges the criticality of software's contribution to hazards and expresses it as a quoted level
ARP 4754A (civil aerospace) allocates a software Development Assurance Level (DAL)
Mil-Std-882E (military) allocates a Level of Rigour, translatable into a DO-178 DAL

title: Principle 2: Preserve Intent During Decomposition
desc: The intent of safety requirements must survive requirement decomposition

body.alt: Burned-out Lufthansa Airbus A320 after the Warsaw runway overrun, 1993
body.caption: Lufthansa Flight 2904, Warsaw, 14 September 1993: thrust reversers and spoilers were locked out because the 'aircraft on ground' logic — a decomposed requirement — didn't match the actual landing conditions.

items: DO-178 strongly emphasizes traceability through all software development stages
Decomposed requirements must be validated, e.g. through requirements review
Recording traceability information is required — but necessary, not sufficient: a written justification of the traceability is what actually preserves intent

title: Principle 3: Satisfy the Requirements
desc: Safety requirements that exist on paper only are worthless

body.alt: Artist rendering of the Mars Polar Lander on the Martian surface
body.caption: Mars Polar Lander, lost on arrival 3 December 1999: the touchdown-detection software satisfied its spec — but a known transient signal on leg deployment shut the engines down 40 meters above the surface.

items: This principle is addressed well by DO-178: it requires verification evidence demonstrating requirements under normal and fault conditions
DO-178 was historically heavily test-focused — DO-178C permits a wider range of verification techniques: analysis, reviews, formal methods, not just testing

title: Principle 4: Identify & Mitigate Hazardous Behavior
desc: The software itself can introduce new failure modes

body.caption: Dhahran, 25 February 1991: a Patriot battery failed to intercept a Scud missile — a floating-point time-accumulation error after 100 hours of continuous operation. 28 soldiers died, 98 were injured.

items: Software design process activities 'could introduce modes of failure into the software or, conversely, preclude others'
In such cases additional data must be defined as derived requirements and fed back to the system safety assessment process
An objective of DO-178 verification is to remove errors that would lead to unacceptable failure conditions
Watch out: the loop back to system safety is where projects often break down in practice

title: Principle 4+1: Confidence Proportionate to Risk
desc: The more the software can hurt, the more evidence you owe

items: Confidence in the implementation of the software safety principles must be commensurate with the software's contribution to system risk
In DO-178C this is captured through the DAL mechanism: the assigned level tailors how many objectives must be demonstrated (with independence, with formal rigor, ...)
Caveat: the system-level analysis determines the DAL but does not necessarily hand the developer derived safety requirements — the level alone is not a specification

title: Case Study: Boeing 777 Fly-By-Wire
desc: What 'confidence commensurate with risk' looks like in hardware and software

items: The Boeing 777 flight control system is a full fly-by-wire (FBW) system with extreme integrity and availability requirements
Triple redundancy for every resource: computing system, electrical power, hydraulic power and communication path
The Primary Flight Computer (PFC) uses triple modular redundancy (TMR) — and additionally N-version diversity
Three similar channels (same part number), each containing three dissimilar computing lanes — different processors, different compilers
Communication runs over the ARINC 629 data bus
One software bug in one lane cannot silently take down the flight controls — that is Principle 4+1 built in metal and code

links.label: Yeh: Triple-Triple Redundant 777 Primary Flight Computer (IEEE)

items: Aviation software lives under DO-178C (airborne), ED-109 (ground/ATM) and ED-153 (air navigation services)
Defects get ~100× more expensive in the field — standards force the work to the front of the lifecycle
91.9% of computer-related accidental deaths come from human-computer interaction, only 2.8% from software errors — safe design means context, requirements, interaction and change control
44% of control-system failures are born in the specification
The 4+1 principles: define safety requirements, preserve intent, satisfy them, mitigate hazardous behavior — with confidence proportionate to risk (DALs)
The Boeing 777 FBW shows the principles physically: triple-triple redundancy, N-version diversity, ARINC 629
